skill.name: using-git-worktrees
skill.description: Guides agents through creating isolated Git worktrees, selecting safe directories, verifying ignore rules, running setup, and checking a clean baseline.
skill.summary: Guides safe Git worktree creation, setup, and baseline verification for isolated feature development.
skill.author: obra

security_audit.summary: Static command findings are mostly true positives because the skill documents shell commands that an agent may execute. No malicious intent, data exfiltration, network beaconing, or prompt injection was found, but dependency installation and test commands can run untrusted project code.

medium_findings.title: Agent-Executed Shell Commands
medium_findings.description: The external command findings are true positives in context. The skill instructs agents to run local git, shell, package manager, and test commands, which is expected for a Git worktree workflow but still executes code on the user machine.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly documented and likely to be executed by an agent. They are ordinary development commands, so the risk is operational rather than evidence of malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Dependency Install and Build Commands May Execute Project Code
medium_findings.description: The setup section directs agents to run npm install, cargo build, pip install, poetry install, and go mod download based on project files. These commands are legitimate setup steps, but they can execute dependency hooks, build scripts, or untrusted code in a compromised repository.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The listed package manager and build commands are direct evidence. The risk depends on the repository contents, but dependency setup is a known execution boundary.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem and Git State Modification
medium_findings.description: The skill tells agents to add a worktree ignore rule, commit the change, create a worktree path, and change directories. This is normal for the workflow, but it modifies repository files and branch state and should require user awareness.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file and git modifications are explicitly described. They are bounded to normal repository workflow and do not show destructive or covert behavior.

low_findings.title: Hidden Directory Matches Are Mostly Benign
low_findings.description: The hidden path findings refer to .worktrees and ~/.config/superpowers/worktrees as storage locations. They do not access secrets or sensitive hidden files, but global hidden-directory use should be visible to users.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations are user-facing worktree directory examples, not covert access to hidden credential files. No read of shell profiles, SSH keys, or tokens was found.

low_findings.title: Shell Redirection Matches Are Benign
low_findings.description: The standard device file access findings are shell stderr redirections to /dev/null. This suppresses expected command errors and does not read or write sensitive device data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only device-file reference is 2>/dev/null in ordinary shell examples. This is a common safe redirection pattern.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography flags do not correspond to any cryptographic operation at the cited lines. No hashing, encryption, or security-sensitive cryptographic algorithm is implemented by this Markdown skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited file is instructional Markdown and contains no crypto API or algorithm implementation. The static signal appears to be a keyword collision.

dangerous_patterns.title: Package Manager and Test Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: Commands such as dependency installation, build, and test execution can run repository-controlled scripts. This should only be used in repositories the user trusts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly instructs execution of setup and test commands. The security impact is contextual but real in untrusted repositories.

dangerous_patterns.title: Automatic Git Ignore and Commit Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends changing .gitignore and committing that change before creating a worktree. This is not malicious, but it changes repository history and should be reviewed.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file modification and commit instruction is explicit. The behavior is legitimate workflow guidance but requires user awareness.

content.value_statement: Feature work can disrupt the current workspace and hide baseline problems. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through safe worktree setup, dependency checks, and test verification.
actual_capabilities: Selects an existing project-local worktree directory when one is available.
Checks CLAUDE.md for a documented worktree directory preference.
Prompts the user when no safe directory convention is found.
Verifies project-local worktree directories are ignored before use.
Creates a new Git worktree and branch for isolated feature work.
Runs detected project setup and baseline test commands before implementation.
limitations: Requires a Git repository that supports worktrees.
May run dependency installation and tests that execute repository code.
Does not resolve failing baseline tests without user direction.
Does not choose a directory automatically when project conventions are missing.

use_cases.title: Start a Feature Branch Safely
use_cases.description: Create a separate worktree before implementing a feature so current edits remain undisturbed.

use_cases.title: Prepare an Agent Workspace
use_cases.description: Give an AI coding assistant a clean branch and verified setup before it changes code.

use_cases.title: Validate Project Baseline
use_cases.description: Run setup and tests in the new worktree so existing failures are visible before new work begins.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Worktree
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new feature.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the using-git-worktrees skill to create an isolated worktree for my next feature branch. Ask before choosing a directory if the project has no convention.

prompt_templates.title: Follow Project Convention
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up a Git worktree for this task. Check existing worktree folders and CLAUDE.md first, then create the branch using the project convention.

prompt_templates.title: Verify Baseline Before Coding
prompt_templates.scenario: Confirm setup and tests before implementation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a worktree for the planned change, install dependencies only when project files require it, run baseline tests, and report any failures before coding.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Agent Implementation Workspace
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the using-git-worktrees workflow to prepare an isolated workspace for agent implementation, including ignore verification, branch creation, setup detection, and clean baseline reporting.

output_examples.input: Create a worktree for an authentication feature.
output_examples.output: A concise status report naming the selected worktree directory, the created branch, setup steps completed, and baseline test result.

output_examples.input: No worktree directory exists in the project.
output_examples.output: A short user prompt asking whether to use a project-local hidden directory or the documented global superpowers directory.
A failure report that separates existing test problems from the planned feature work and asks whether to investigate or continue.

best_practices: Use this skill before feature work that should not touch the current workspace.
Review dependency installation and test commands before running them in untrusted repositories.
Keep worktree directory conventions documented in the repository for repeatable setup.
anti_patterns: Do not create project-local worktrees before verifying the directory is ignored.
Do not continue implementation after failing baseline tests without user approval.
Do not use this skill to run setup commands in repositories you do not trust.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps an AI assistant create an isolated Git worktree, prepare the project, and verify a clean baseline before coding.

faq.question: Does it modify my repository?
faq.answer: Yes. It may add an ignore rule, commit that rule, create a branch, and create a worktree directory.

faq.question: Why does it check ignore rules?
faq.answer: It checks ignore rules to reduce the chance that worktree contents are accidentally tracked by the main repository.

faq.question: Can it run package managers?
faq.answer: Yes. It can run setup commands when project files indicate Node.js, Rust, Python, or Go dependencies.

faq.question: Is it safe for untrusted repositories?
faq.answer: Use caution. Dependency installation, builds, and tests can execute repository-controlled code.

faq.question: Which assistants can use it?
faq.answer: The report marks it as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
